currency.name: Omani Rial
currency.budgetNote: Recommended: 10 OMR (the Omani Rial is one of the strongest currencies on Earth)

mapQuestions.question: Which continent is Oman on?
mapQuestions.answer: Asia, on the south-east corner of the Arabian Peninsula, with the Indian Ocean to the south and the Gulf of Oman to the north 🌍

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of Oman?
mapQuestions.answer: Muscat, on the Gulf coast. About 1.4 million people. A low-rise city of white buildings nestled between the sea and the brown Hajar Mountains.

mapQuestions.question: What language does Oman speak?
mapQuestions.answer: Arabic (official). English is also widely spoken, especially in tourism, business and signage. Swahili is spoken by Omani families with East African roots.

mapQuestions.question: How long is Oman's coastline?
mapQuestions.answer: About 3,165 km, one of the longest coastlines of any country in the Arabian Peninsula. The Indian Ocean, the Gulf of Oman and the Arabian Sea all wrap around Oman.

mapQuestions.question: How is Oman different from its neighbours?
mapQuestions.answer: Oman is famous for being quietly stable, peaceful, and unusually well-kept. The country has invested heavily in preserving its old forts, towns and traditional Arab architecture instead of building tall.

foods.name: Shuwa
foods.description: The Omani feast dish: lamb or goat marinated in spices, wrapped in banana leaves and roasted for 24 to 48 HOURS in a pit oven underground. Served on big rice platters at Eid celebrations and weddings.

foods.name: Mashuai
foods.description: Whole spit-roasted kingfish (or other big firm Omani fish) served on a bed of lemon rice. Coastal Omani classic.

foods.name: Halwa
foods.description: A sticky-sweet semolina-and-rosewater dessert, deeply orange-brown, flavoured with cardamom, saffron and almonds. Served with strong coffee at every Omani gathering.

foods.name: Khubz rakhal
foods.description: Paper-thin Omani flatbread cooked on a curved metal dome. Folded over fillings or used to scoop curries.

foods.name: Kahwa Omani
foods.description: Light, fragrant Omani coffee flavoured with cardamom (and sometimes rosewater and saffron), served in small handle-less cups with fresh dates.

foods.name: Camel meat
foods.description: Lean, slightly sweet camel meat is a traditional Omani Bedouin food, often slow-cooked in stews. Sometimes served at weddings.

scavengerItems.label: The Sultan Qaboos Grand Mosque in Muscat (with the world's second-biggest hand-woven carpet)
A round Omani fort (Nizwa, Nakhal, Bahla) with thick mud-brick walls
A camel on a Sharqiya Sands dune
An "aflaj" (a centuries-old underground water channel) feeding a green date-palm oasis
Untouched white-sand beaches with no one on them (Oman has hundreds of km)
A green sea turtle laying eggs at Ras al-Jinz (May to October)
A "wadi" (a dry-bottomed canyon with sudden bright-blue pools after rain)
A man in a "dishdasha" (long white robe) with a "kuma" (embroidered cap)
A woman in a colourful Omani "kandura" dress with embroidered cuffs
Coral reefs at the Daymaniyat Islands off the north coast
A "souq" with shops selling silver "khanjar" daggers, frankincense and rosewater
The "khareef" (monsoon mist) covering the Dhofar hills green in summer

geoMatches.description: The capital, on the Gulf of Oman. White low-rise city wedged between the sea and brown mountains. Sultan Qaboos Grand Mosque, the Royal Opera House, Mutrah Souq.
The old interior capital of Oman, with a famous round fort, a date-palm oasis around it, and a Friday-morning goat market that has run for centuries.
A spectacular red-and-golden dune sea east of Muscat. Camp under the stars in a Bedouin desert camp, ride camels, watch sunrises over the dunes.
The "Green Mountain", a high plateau at 2,000+ m where rose-water roses, pomegranates and walnut trees grow on terraced gardens carved into the cliffs.
A southern coastal region that turns BRIGHT green in summer because of the "khareef" monsoon mist (June to September). Beaches, banana farms, frankincense trees.
A bright-blue canyon swimming spot in the eastern Hajar mountains. Wade through pools, swim through narrow gorges, and pass through a hidden cave to a waterfall.

senses.smell: Frankincense burning in shops, cardamom in Omani coffee, hot Hajar sand at midday, fresh oranges in a Nizwa souq, rosewater on a Jebel Akhdar terrace…
senses.hear: The call to prayer drifting across Muscat at dawn, "Marhaba!" greetings, the slap of an Omani sandal on white marble, the rhythmic chant of a Mutrah dhow workshop, the silence of the Sharqiya dunes at night…
senses.taste: Sweet sticky halwa, slow-roasted shuwa lamb falling off the bone, soft date with bitter coffee, lemon-fresh fish on rice, smoky cardamom kahwa…
senses.feel: Warm Gulf water (28°C even in winter), hot dune sand under bare feet, the cool inside of a clay-walled fort, smooth white marble of a mosque floor, the soft heavy weave of an Omani rug…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: Why is Muscat so low-rise?
stories.body: Most Gulf capitals (Dubai, Doha, Abu Dhabi, Riyadh) have raced to build the tallest skyscrapers possible. Muscat went the other way. A law passed by the late Sultan Qaboos requires that almost no building in Muscat can be more than about 7 stories tall, and that buildings must be in traditional Omani colours and styles (mostly white, sand or cream). The result is a calm, low-rise capital wedged between the sea and brown mountains, with mosque domes as the tallest features. The Sultan Qaboos Grand Mosque is the most spectacular building in the country: it can hold 20,000 worshippers, and the central prayer hall has the second-biggest hand-woven carpet in the world (4,200 square metres, made by 600 weavers over 4 years).

stories.question: What is an "aflaj"?
stories.body: For around 1,500 years, Omanis have used a clever underground irrigation system called "aflaj" (singular: "falaj") to bring water from mountain springs to villages and date-palm oases. The aflaj are gently sloped channels, sometimes kilometres long, often partly underground to slow evaporation. Each village shares the water on a strict rotation, with time-shares measured by a sundial or the position of certain stars. UNESCO put the Omani aflaj system on the World Heritage list in 2006. About 4,000 falaj systems are still in use today, watering palms and crops in an otherwise dry country.
stories.thinkingQuestion: A community has shared the same water-share system for 1,500 years. What in your own community has been done the same way for that long?

stories.question: What is frankincense?
stories.body: Frankincense is a fragrant resin that drips from the trunks of "Boswellia" trees when their bark is cut. Frankincense trees grow almost exclusively in the dry hills of southern Oman (the Dhofar region around Salalah), parts of Yemen, and the Horn of Africa. The Dhofar variety is considered the best on Earth. For over 3,000 years, frankincense was one of the most valuable goods in the ancient world: traded by camel caravan from southern Arabia to Egypt, Greece, Rome and India for use in religion, perfume and medicine. It was so precious that the three wise men gave the baby Jesus frankincense as a gift. UNESCO put the Land of Frankincense on its World Heritage list in 2000. You can visit the original frankincense trees.

stories.question: Who was Sultan Qaboos?
stories.body: When Sultan Qaboos bin Said al Said took power in Oman in 1970, the country had almost no schools, no hospitals, almost no paved roads, and very few even basic services. Over the next 50 years he transformed Oman: free education for all citizens, universal healthcare, modern infrastructure, religious tolerance, the most beautiful Arabian-style architecture preserved in any Gulf country. He was widely respected across the Middle East as a peacemaker, often quietly mediating between feuding neighbours. When he died in 2020, the entire country went into 40 days of mourning. The current Sultan, Haitham bin Tariq, is his cousin and has continued his approach.

stories.question: How do you watch sea turtles lay eggs?
stories.body: At Ras al-Jinz, the easternmost point of Arabia, around 20,000 endangered green sea turtles come ashore EVERY YEAR to lay their eggs in the sand. From May to October, especially at night, you can join guided beach walks to watch a 1.5-metre female turtle haul herself slowly up the beach, dig a deep nest with her flippers, and lay up to 100 ping-pong-ball-sized eggs. Six weeks later, the babies hatch and run for the sea. The Ras al-Jinz reserve has run a strict conservation programme for decades, with most visitor numbers limited and red-light torches only (white light disorients hatchlings). It is one of the best places in the world to see this happening.

convoQuestions.question: Best food in Oman so far?
Muscat, desert, mountain, beach, which has felt the most "Oman"?
Did you spend a night in a Bedouin desert camp?
How does Oman feel different from other Gulf countries you have seen or heard about?
Aflaj water systems have run for 1,500 years on a strict community schedule. How does that compare to how your community shares resources?

wordSearch.hint: Omani foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: Green sea turtle
animals.description: Lays eggs by the thousand at Ras al-Jinz on the east coast (May to October). One of the world's most important turtle nesting sites.

animals.name: Dromedary camel
animals.description: Across the Sharqiya Sands and the interior. Sometimes raced at the camel race-track outside Muscat.

animals.name: Reef fish
animals.description: Bright clownfish, parrotfish and angelfish around the Daymaniyat Islands off Oman's north coast.

animals.name: Arabian oryx
animals.description: A pale-white antelope with long straight horns, once extinct in the wild and reintroduced to Oman's Jiddat al-Harasis reserve.

animals.name: Wild Arabian tahr
animals.description: A small wild goat found only in the Hajar Mountains of Oman and the UAE. Around 2,000 left.

animals.name: Spinner dolphin
animals.description: Plays in big pods off the Muscat and Musandam coasts. Pinks-spotted patterns visible underwater.

animals.name: Egyptian vulture
animals.description: A small white-and-black vulture nesting on Hajar Mountain cliffs.
